coredata.dc:title: 3D printed self-calibrating on-site sensing platform based on bimodal excitation carbon dots for visual 4-nitrophenol detection by means of the localization of inner filter effect and pH regulation
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Guoqiang
Zheng, Qi
Li, Tingting
Zhang, Ting
Tang, Siyuan
Li, Lintao
Gao, Sineng
Tang, Yan
Chen, Da
coredata.dc:description: For facile and rapid detection of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), we synthesize nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) through microwave radiation treatment using melamine and o-phenylenediamine as precursors. We employed N-CDs as an ingenious ratiometric fluorescence sensor via a novel bimodal excitation behavior. As the concentration of N-CDs increases, the broad excitation peak splits into two excitation peaks. The blue-shift phenomenon appears at the short-wavelength excitation peak, while the long-wavelength excitation peak presents the red-shift. Consequently, 4-NP can be sensitively detected by N-CDs owing to the localization of inner filter effect (L-IFE) with the detection limit of 83 nM ranging from 0.083 to 80 µM. Moreover, a self-calibrating on-site sensing platform constructed by 3D printing technique realizes the visual 4-NP detection in actual samples based on bimodal excitation behavior and RGB analysis. Our work not only presents a novel perspective for visual 4-NP sensing, but also renders the integration of 3D printing technology and the CDs industry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bimodal excitation N-CDs
Ratiometric fluorescence strategy
4-NP
The localized inner filter effect
pH regulation
3D printed self-calibrating sensing platform

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: 4-NP sensing based on bimodal excitation ratiometric fluorescence method
Visual 4-NP detection using 3D printed self-calibrating on-site sensing platform
Design of 3D printed self-calibrating on-site sensing platform and sensing mechanism
Synthesis and characterizations of bimodal excitation N-CDs
Mechanism for fluorescence sensing and pH regulation toward 4-NP
Performance of excitation-based ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor for 4-NP detection
Determination of 4-NP based on 3D printed self-calibrating on-site sensing device

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the fabrication process (a-b) and photos (c) of 3D printed self-calibrating on-site sensing device for monitoring 4-NP.

(a) TEM image of N-CDs (inset: the corresponding size distribution and HR-TEM image of N-CDs). (b) The AFM image and height analysis of N-CDs. (c) FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra for C 1 s (d), N 1 s (e) and O 1 s (f) of N-CDs. (g) The UV–vis absorption, PLE and PL spectra of N-CDs (inset: the photos of N-CDs under visible and 365 nm illuminants). (h) Schematic diagram of the real experimental absorption, excitation and emission of N-CDs. (ic: internal conversion, VR: vibration relaxation). (i) PL spectra of N-CDs under different wavelength excitations.

Excitation-emission matrix graph maps of N-CDs in the presence of 4-NP at pH = 6 (a) and pH = 8 (b). (c) UV–vis absorbance of 4-NP solutions in the pH range of 3.5–9.5. (d) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs, 4-NP, N-CDs/4-NP and physical addition of N-CDs and 4-NP (N-CDs + 4-NP). (e) Fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs and N-CDs + 4-NP compound aqueous solutions. (f) The energy level diagram of bimodal excitation N-CDs and 4-NP.

(a) PLE spectra of N-CDs under different concentrations (0.02–1.2 mg‧mL−1). (b) PL intensity of N-CDs at different pH with and without 4-NP under the excitation of 390 nm. (c) The fluorescence quenching of N-CDs at various incubation time with 4-NP. (d) Selectivity of N-CDs under the excitation of 390 nm. (e) PLE spectra of N-CDs with different concentrations of 4-NP. Plot of the PLE intensity ratio (I468/I390 ) versus 4-NP concentration in the range of 0–150 µM (f) and linear fitting (g).

(a) Flow charts for the smartphone-assisted device to analyze the 4-NP concentration. (b) Fluorescence images of 4-NP sensing. (c) Linear correlation of the Sum2/Sum1 ratio values and 4-NP concentrations from 0.115 to 70 μM. (d) Images for practical application in 4-NP sensing.

Schematic illustration of the synthetic process of the bimodal excitation N-CDs (a), the principle of 4-NP sensing (b) and application for actual sample detection using the 3D printed self-calibrating on-site sensing platform (c).

head.ce:title: 3D printed self-calibrating on-site sensing platform based on bimodal excitation carbon dots for visual 4-nitrophenol detection by means of the localization of inner filter effect and pH regulation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Research Institute of Advanced Technologies, Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Research Institute of Advanced Technologies
Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Research Institute of Advanced Technologies, Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Ningbo Polytechnic, Ningbo, Zhejiang 315800, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Ningbo Polytechnic, Ningbo, Zhejiang 315800, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: For facile and rapid detection of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), we synthesize nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) through microwave radiation treatment using melamine and o-phenylenediamine as precursors. We employed N-CDs as an ingenious ratiometric fluorescence sensor via a novel bimodal excitation behavior. As the concentration of N-CDs increases, the broad excitation peak splits into two excitation peaks. The blue-shift phenomenon appears at the short-wavelength excitation peak, while the long-wavelength excitation peak presents the red-shift. Consequently, 4-NP can be sensitively detected by N-CDs owing to the localization of inner filter effect (L-IFE) with the detection limit of 83 nM ranging from 0.083 to 80 µM. Moreover, a self-calibrating on-site sensing platform constructed by 3D printing technique realizes the visual 4-NP detection in actual samples based on bimodal excitation behavior and RGB analysis. Our work not only presents a novel perspective for visual 4-NP sensing, but also renders the integration of 3D printing technology and the CDs industry.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, a significant threat to water quality and food security has resulted from the tremendous increase in the use of nitrated phenols, which has turned out to be the primary origin of disease induction and environmental pollution (Cheng et al., 2021). Among the various nitrated phenols, 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) has been mainly used as intermediates of pesticide, medicine, dye and other fine chemicals. Besides, it is also classified as a toxic priority pollutant for the environment. Some research shows that the toxicity of 4-NP can cause cyto-embryotoxic and mutagenic effects in mammals even at a low concentration (Li et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2022). In this way, the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) of the United States has stipulated the maximum accepted content of 0.43 µM for potable water (González-Calabuig et al., 2018). Accordingly, it is highly imperative to construct a simple, efficient and sensitive strategy for 4-NP analysis. Currently, among the various detection techniques developed (such as ultra-high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry, high-performance liquid chromatography, fluorescence spectroscopy and gas chromatography-mass, etc.), fluorescence spectroscopy has shown great merits in aspects of the cost effective, sensitivity, feasibility and simplicity (Harrison et al., 2005; Kitanovski et al., 2012; Mehdinia et al., 2014; Bogireddy et al., 2020). Consequently, neoteric design of fluorometry with high sensitivity and selectivity for 4-NP detection is always a research focus in this field.

Carbon dots (CDs), known as carbon nanomaterials, have been studied extensively in a variety of domains on account of their excellent cytocompatibility, tunable optical properties, simple synthesis routes, cost-effectiveness and versatility for further functionalization (Cao et al., 2022; Ding et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020; Gong et al., 2019). At present, nitrogen-atoms doping is a general and effective strategy because of their special superiorities. Since N as the close neighbors of the carbon in the periodic table has similar atomic radius to carbon, therefore it can modify the properties of CDs effectively (Gao et al., 2017). In addition, the introduction of N atoms may create new energy levels and change electric potential in CDs, which change the chemical reactivity of CDs (Ritter and Lyding, 2009). Therefore, it is meaningful to explore CDs with higher nitrogen content. Moreover, these preponderances enable CDs to be a superior fluorescence material for the preparation of nanosensors for monitoring 4-NP. For instance, Liao et al. utilized nitrogen-doped CDs with blue fluorescence emission for highly selective detection of 4-NP through the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Yu et al., 2017). Li et al. fabricated a dual-emissive neuron cell-analogous carbon-based probe for ratiometric detection of 4-NP based on a π-π stacking quenching mechanism (Zhou et al., 2014). Fu et al. designed a visible-near infrared ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor (carbon dots@zeolitic imidazolate framework-8) for 4-NP detection using emission signals (Fu et al., 2022). There into, the majority of fluorescence analyses are based on single signal, which are susceptible to interference from precision and stability of instrument, background noises and the concentration of fluorescence probe. In comparison, the development of CDs with multiple signals variation capability is highly desirable, which can be designed as a ratiometric fluorescence sensor against environmental disturbance effects because of its a built-in correction. In other words, this method uses two fluorescence signals at different wavelengths which can be simultaneously monitored and their intensity ratio is used for the quantitative analysis. This pattern introduces an effective internal referencing and excludes the fluctuation of light excitation intensity which improves the sensitivity of the detection. However, most of them generally need the chemical conjugation or surface modification between the acceptor and donor, since the quenching efficiency resulting from the FRET or electron transfer (ET) is associated with their distance (Hao et al., 2016; Li et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2022). In contrast, constructing sensors based on the inner filter effect (IFE) induced fluorescence quenching does not require any above operations, since IFE occurs so long as the absorber absorbance pairs well with the fluorophore excitation or emission (Tang et al., 2022; Tang et al., 2022; Hu et al., 2022). On the other hand, the overwhelming majority of fluorescence sensors ever reported rely on their emission signals, while the fluorescence spectroscopy can also gain other fluorescence information, like excitation information, that is easily neglected (Liu et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2021). The ratiometric fluorescence sensing strategy based on excitation signals toward 4-NP has rarely been reported. Thereby, fabricating an IFE-based ratiometric sensor relying on excitation information is challenging and greatly meaningful for 4-NP determination.

Meanwhile, with the rapid development of additive manufacturing technology, it is assured that 3D printing, a convenient and efficient preparation method, will undoubtedly be employed widely in daily life, benefitting from the simplified preparation process, precise structural design and raw materials diversity (Zhu et al., 2020). In this regard, to maximize detection performance and accelerate the commercial manufacturing of advanced sensors, it is promising and profound for the construction of hand-held and consumer-friendly sensors incorporating the excellent sensing performance of CDs with 3D printing (Santos et al., 2022; García-Astrain et al., 2020; Abdalla and Patel, 2021).

In this work, we focus on developing an ingenious IFE-based bimodal excitation ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor and designing a self-calibrating on-site sensing platform for selective and sensitive detection of 4-NP. Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) are primarily prepared by using microwave radiation using o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and melamine (Scheme 1 (a)). The as-synthesized N-CDs present a novel bimodal excitation behavior. Namely, the distance of dual-excitation is simply modulated by the concentration of N-CDs. Accordingly, resulting from the unique IFE relationship between N-CDs and 4-NP, the short-wavelength excitation of N-CDs is quenched with the introduction of 4-NP. Meanwhile, the long-wavelength excitation faintly fluctuates, which is almost constant in contrast with the short-wavelength excitation change. In this manner, we select the long-wavelength and short-wavelength excitations as reference and response signals for ratiometric sensing, respectively, to detect 4-NP with a low detection limit (LOD) of 83 nM in the range of 0.083–80 µM. Concomitantly, a 3D printed self-calibrating on-site sensing platform is developed and successfully devoted to practical application owing to the noticeable brightness changes under two different excitations (Scheme 1(b)). In addition, the 4-NP content is rapidly determined with satisfactory recoveries of 104.3–110.8% and relative standard deviation of 2.68–4.70% using several water samples as practical samples (Scheme 1(c)). There is a good fusion of 3D printing technology and ingenious sensing strategy. Thus, the potential application of the developed bimodal excitation ratiometric fluorescence sensor combined with 3D printing technology will aid in developing a rapid and inexpensive strategy for visual sensing of 4-NP in the aspect of foodstuff and environmental hygiene issues.

Melamine, OPD, N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), BaCl2, AgNO3, NaCl, K2CO3, CaCl2, CoCl2, HgCl2, NiCl2, MnCl2, FeCl2, PbCl2, CuSO4, CdCl2, ZnCl2, SnCl2, NaI, NaBr, NaNO3, Na2CO3, Na2SO4, NaClO4, NaOH, HCl, m-phenylenediamine (m-PD), p-phenylenediamine (p-PD), 4-chlorophenol (4-CP), methylbenzene (TOL), aniline (AN), hydroquinone (HQ), 4-nitrotoluene (4-NT), 2-nitrophenol (2-NP), 3-nitrophenol (3-NP), 4-NP, all amino acids and other chemical substances were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents supplied by commercial suppliers were of analytical reagent grade, and used without further purification. Deionized water obtained from a Milli-R04 purification system was used throughout all experiments.

First, 0.108 g of OPD and 0.113 g of melamine were dissolved in 20 mL DMF, and the solution system was ultrasonicated for 40 min. Subsequently, the mixed solution was poured into a microwave digestion tank and treated at a microwave power of 250 W for 30 min to obtain a dark brown solution. After being lowered to room temperature, the dark brown solution containing N-CDs was obtained. The obtained solution was purified by passing through a 0.22 µm filter membrane. Finally, the product was obtained after vacuum drying for 36 h.

The morphologies of N-CDs were observed using transmission electron microscope (TEM, Hitachi H-8100). The height of N-CDs was characterized by using an atomic force microscope (AFM, Cypher S, Oxford Instruments). An X-ray diffractometer (PANalytical X’Pert Pro MPD) was used to record powder XRD patterns. The surface chemical states of N-CDs were characterized using Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR, Nicolet FT-IR-iS5) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Axis Ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer). The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra (photoluminescence (PL) and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra) were obtained using UV-5800 spectrophotometer and PerkinElmer LS55 luminescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer Instruments, U.K.), respectively. The time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of analytes were measured using a FL3-111 spectrometer (Horiba, France) at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 4-NP sensing based on bimodal excitation ratiometric fluorescence method
ce:section.ce:para: As a representative experiment, 10 mg of N-CDs were dissolved in 20 mL of Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer solution (0.04 mol‧L−1 phosphoric acid, boric acid and acetic acid solutions) via ultrasonication for 4 min. Then, 1 mL of N-CDs solution and 4-NP at a final concentration range from 0 to 150 µM were added to a centrifuge tube. Added the pH = 8 BR buffer solution until the final mixture volume was 2 mL. After incubation of 2 min, the PLE spectra of the mixed solutions were monitored at 565 nm emission wavelength at room temperature. The selectivity of 4-NP sensing was confirmed by adding various amino acids, common ions and similar substance solutions (including L-asparagine, L-proline, L-alanine, L-serine, Ba2+, Ag+, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Sn2+, Cl−, I−, Br−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, ClO4 −, m-PD, p-PD, 4-CP, TOL, AN, HQ, 4-NT, 2-NP and 3-NP) following by a similar way.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual 4-NP detection using 3D printed self-calibrating on-site sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: For the qualitative and visual detection of 4-NP in actual samples, 3D printed self-calibrating on-site sensing platform was employed. First, 4-NP solution at different concentrations was dropped into the sample pad. After equilibration of 2 min, analytes infiltrated through two parallel hydrophilic channels to the control and test lines. Under the two different wavelength irradiations, through the naked eye, the notable differentiated brightness of the control and test lines was observed, indicating the self-calibrating result. The quenching degree in the fluorescence of test line corresponded to standard samples, and these results were converted to RGB values for qualitative and semiquantitative detection. Furthermore, to estimate the authentic applicability of the proposed sensing platform, various water samples (sewage, tap water and Yongjiang river water) have been selected as the research objects. Moreover, these samples were tested in the same way.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of 3D printed self-calibrating on-site sensing platform and sensing mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Probes dispersed in solvent for analyte sensing feature the property of inconvenience and susceptibility to interference. Hence, we adopt paper-based microfluidic analytical devices (μPADs) because of their low cost and ease of use, which plays a role in medical diagnosis, environmental monitoring, biochemical analysis, food security and other fields. Details of the preparation of the rapid diagnostic test can be found in the supporting information. Subsequently, aiming at an efficient, rapid and economical detection of 4-NP, we utilize the 3D printing technology to fabricate a self-calibrating on-site sensing platform that combines μPADs immobilized by N-CDs and two miniature lamps of different wavelengths to analyze the content of 4-NP (Fig. 1 (a)). For 3D printing technology, the geometrical structures of objects are designed, and these files are saved in the standard triangle language (.stl) format. Subsequently, the model is printed layer by layer deposition of material directly from the computer-aided design (CAD) in vertical and horizontal planes. The decomposition schema of the CAD models is displayed in Fig. 1(b), and the realistic photos are shown in Fig. 1(c). It is observed that the platform is equipped with two channels toward control and test cells, and one sample inlet. After transfused in the sample inlet, the analyte flows into the control and test cells driven by capillary force. As for the function of self-calibrating, it means that the detection results are valid only if the control line is in a turn-on state, and can be gained for semi-quantitative analysis through RGB (RGB value = Red + Green + Blue) analysis APP of smartphone, while their ratio value of Sum1 and Sum2 (Sum1 and Sum2 represent the RGB values at excitations of 390 nm and 468 nm, respectively) is used as a parameter for quantification. In addition, it is worth mentioning that the crafted loadable structure can be adapted to repeatability detection if only to change μPADs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterizations of bimodal excitation N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to prepare CDs with higher nitrogen content, melamine and OPD are determined as precursors. For melamine, it is a typical organic base and the trimer of cyanamide with a 1, 3, 5-triazine skeleton, which is considered as an ideal precursor for nitrogen-doped carbon materials since it contains 67 % N atoms by mass and the three amine groups on it can easily react with amino on the surface of OPD (Ding et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2012; Roushani and Abdi, 2014). Meanwhile, the nitrogen configuration on the six-membered ring easily forms graphite nitrogen during synthesis. It can lead to the creation of new energy levels in CDs, reducing the energy of electronic transition, which realizes the change of the wavelength (Sim et al., 2020). Consequently, we have prepared N-CDs by using microwave radiation using OPD and melamine and characterized them.

The morphology and size of N-CDs are observed on the TEM image (Fig. 2 (a)), which illustrate that N-CDs are uniform spherical with an average particle size of 3.5 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image in the inset of Fig. 2(a) presents lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.24 nm which corresponds to the (100) in-plane spacing of graphene (Ding et al., 2023). The AFM image (Fig. 2(b)) shows that the topographic height of N-CDs is mainly distributed in 1.0–4.1 nm, which indicates that N-CDs are successfully synthesized and primarily consist of a few layers of graphene-like sheets. Meanwhile, the DLS measurement (Fig. S1) reveals an average hydrodynamic size of 3.3 nm, which is in line with the result of TEM analysis. From the XRD pattern (Fig. S2), N-CDs have a broad apparent diffraction peak around 22.1°, which is assigned to be the (100) facet of graphite, validating that the structure of N-CDs is crystalline (Ding et al., 2018). Meanwhile, FT-IR and XPS analyses are carried out to characterize the chemical constituents and functional groups of N-CDs. Fig. 2(c) displays that the peaks at 1372 cm−1 and 1596 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of C–N/C=N, demonstrating the N doping of N-CDs. The bands at 1107–1153 cm−1 and 1627–1659 cm−1 are assigned to the C–O and C=O function groups (Ma et al., 2022; Park et al., 2019). The C=C stretching vibration at 1581 cm−1 indicates that N-CDs contain polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (Gnanamoorthy et al., 2021). The region of 3167–3456 cm−1 confirms the existence of O-H/N-H (Wang et al., 2021; Gu et al., 2022). In the XPS full scan spectrum (Fig. S3), three predominant peaks at 285.13 eV, 392 eV and 531.97 eV belong to C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s, and it shows that the relative levels of elements are 56.8% C, 38.3 % N and 4.9% O, respectively. As disclosed in Fig. 2(d), the high-resolution C 1 s spectrum is deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.3 eV, 285.5 eV and 287.6 eV, relating to C–C/C=C, C–O/C–N and C=O/C=N, respectively (Tang et al., 2022; Hu et al., 2017). The N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2(e)) displays four components located at 398.2 eV, 398.5 eV, 398.9 eV and 399.3 eV, corresponding to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, amino N and graphitic N bonds (Zhang et al., 2019). The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2(f)) shows the existence of C=O at 531.7 eV and C-O at 532.6 eV (Sima et al., 2022). The above results verify that N-CDs are oxygen-functionalized and doped with N, leading to novel optical characteristic and good dispersity in water.

To study the optical characteristics of N-CDs, the UV–vis and fluorescence spectra are measured. Fig. 2(g) shows that N-CDs have two absorption bands (purple curve): the band at 275 nm originates from the π-π* transitions of the aromatic C=C bonds. The other broader band in the 405–475 nm region results from the n-π* transitions of nitrogen/oxygen-related states located on the N-CDs surface (Wang et al., 2020). Simultaneously, the broad excitation band in the 350–500 nm region contains two excitation peaks (390 nm and 468 nm), and single emission peak occurs at 565 nm upon the optimal excitation. N-CDs solution in Fig. 2(g) inset displays a pellucid and uniform system under daylight, while emitting an intensive yellow fluorescence by 365 nm irradiation. Afterward, while further increasing the concentration of N-CDs from 0.02 to 1.2 mg‧mL−1, the bimodal excitation phenomenon occurs in Fig. S4, showing the distance between two excitation peaks prolong concomitantly. It implies an energy-level rearrangement of N-CDs with the concentration changing, which is associated closely with the structural characteristics. Combined with the FT-IR and XPS data, introducing N and O atoms may create new energy levels and change electric potential in N-CDs, resulting in distinct bimodal excitation states on the surface of N-CDs (Fig. 2(h)) (Jiang et al., 2020; Liao et al., 2021). Moreover, as shown in Fig. 2(i), the emission of N-CDs shows a typical excitation-independent behavior. During the excitation wavelength varied from 370 to 490 nm, the emission shows a stable yellow emission. In a follow-up study, the fluorescence stabilities of N-CDs under different saline solutions and time conditions are evaluated. As depicted in Fig. S5, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs changes negligibly under the concentrated saline conditions, and N-CDs remain favorable photostability when illuminated for 15 h under UV excitation. Thus, this nanosensor enables to tolerate many harsh environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for fluorescence sensing and pH regulation toward 4-NP
ce:section.ce:para:
In this sensing system, the short-wavelength excitation of N-CDs is quenched in the existence of 4-NP, while the long-wavelength excitation is used as a reference. Interestingly, Fig. 3 (a-b) depicts the 3D mapping of N-CDs in the presence of 4-NP at pH 6 and 8: the first characteristics peak (PLE = 390 nm, PL = 565 nm) nearly keeps in accord with that in the absence of 4-NP at pH 6, whereas it drops at pH 8. By contrast, the second characteristics peak (PLE = 468 nm, PL = 565 nm) still remains stable at pH 6 and 8. In order to understand the internal mechanism of the fluorescence response, a series of experiments are carried out to investigate the interaction between 4-NP and N-CDs. According to the abovementioned FT-IR and XPS measurements, the hydrophilic groups (–OH, –COOH and –NH2) exist on the surface of the as-prepared N-CDs, while there are some hydroxyl and amino groups in 4-NP molecules. Hydrogen bonds will be formed as long as they are mixed. The formation of hydrogen bonds between the N-CDs and 4-NP enables the N-CDs target 4-NP with high selectivity and sensitivity for the determination of 4-NP. Thus, to evaluate the interaction strength between N-CDs and 4-NP, the binding constants (K) of 4-NP (4.47 × 103 M−1) are calculated (Fig. S6), and their values are much higher than other ions, according to the Benesi-Hildebrand equation (1): (Yang et al., 2022)
(1)
1 /
( I +
I 0
)
= 1 /
[ K
(
I
max
-
I 0
)
C ]
+ 1 /
(
I
max
-
I 0
)
where I0 , Imax and I denote the PL intensities without and with the existence of 4-NP at a saturated concentration and an immediate concentration, respectively. C is the 4-NP concentration. This result manifests that the sensing mechanism may be associated with the strong hydrogen-bonding.

In addition, the UV–vis absorption spectra of 4-NP at different pH are monitored. As shown in Fig. 3(c), the absorption spectra of 4-NP are regulated by means of pH changing, in which the shift transition results from association and dissociation of H+ from hydroxyl (Ahmed et al., 2015). In accordance with the strong electron-withdrawing property of nitro-group, 4-NP is a weak acid and exists in the form of the molecule at acidic conditions (Johnson et al., 2013; Qu et al., 2017). In contrast, under alkaline conditions, the H+ of 4-NP is captured by OH– to form the oxygen anion, thereby increasing the conjugation degree of conjugated systems and inducing the bathochromic shift of the absorption band. Since the absorption spectrum of 4-NP shifts from 320 to 400 nm at pH 8, it greatly enhances the overlap area between the short-wavelength excitation of N-CDs and 4-NP absorbance. Therefore, the sensing system can be realized by means of pH regulation. Meanwhile, IFE or FRET is inferred to be the dominating response mechanism based on the localized overlap of spectra. To prove this assumption, we study the absorption spectra of N-CDs, 4-NP and the mixture of N-CDs/4-NP. Fig. 3(d) shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs, 4-NP, N-CDs/4-NP and N-CDs + 4-NP (the sum of N-CDs curve and 4-NP curve). It can be observed that the curve of N-CDs/4-NP completely overlaps with the curve of N-CDs + 4-NP, which indicates that there no new compound formed or adhered to the surface of N-CDs (Hu et al., 2023). Furtherly, the fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs and N-CDs/4-NP mixed solution are obtained in Fig. 3(e). The negligible variation of average PL lifetimes (Table S1) and spectral overlap reveal that the FRET is ruled out and IFE is the dominating quenching mechanism in this system. Especially, on account of the localized overlap between the N-CDs excitation and 4-NP absorbance, L-IFE is identified as the dominating quenching cause. The energy level diagram of bimodal excitation N-CDs and 4-NP is presented in Fig. 3(f). Thus, the mechanism for 4-NP determination originates from L-IFE, as well as hydrogen-bonding interaction, contributing to the pre-eminent specificity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of excitation-based ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor for 4-NP detection
ce:section.ce:para: In order to conduct the experiments under the optimum circumstances, the pivotal parameters related to the sensing performance are optimized. In the beginning, the effect of N-CDs concentration on ratiometric sensing performance is assessed. Generally, optical features are associated with the concentration of N-CDs. Moreover, IFE occurs dramatically when the absorber absorbance pairs well with the fluorophore excitation (Tang et al., 2022). Thus, by regulating the concentration of N-CDs, the distance of the two excitation peaks can be controlled accurately, resulting in the mere overlap between the absorbance of 4-NP and the short-wavelength excitation of N-CDs (Fig. 4 (a)). In this manner, we select the stable long-wavelength PLE as reference signal and the turn-off short-wavelength PLE as response signal, respectively, to detect 4-NP in a ratiometric method. According to the PLE spectra of N-CDs at different concentrations, 0.6 mg‧mL−1 is chosen as the optimal concentration of N-CDs for the ratiometric sensing of 4-NP.

The selectivity of N-CDs is demonstrated by some interfering substances existing in the environment, such as molecules (L-asparagine, L-proline, L-alanine and L-serine), cations (Ba2+, Ag+, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Zn2+ and Sn2+), anions (Cl−, I−, Br−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, SO4 2− and ClO4 -) and similar compounds (m-PD, p-PD, 4-CP, TOL, AN, HQ, 4-NT, 2-NP and 3-NP). As depicted in Fig. 4(d), only 4-NP induces distinct PLE changes, while others generate an inconsiderable effect under the excitation of 390 nm. On the contrary, the fluorescence of N-CDs in the existence or nonexistence of interfering substances at an excitation of 468 nm is indeed difficult to distinguish the difference (Fig. S8). These results indicate the pre-eminent specificity of our proposed sensor for detecting 4-NP.

Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence spectra of the N-CDs/4-NP system are studied. Fig. 4(e) illustrates the short-wavelength excitation intensity decreases continuously, whereas that of short-wavelength excitation has an ignorable quenching effect, and the value of I468/I390 of N-CDs increases linearly (Y = 0.1269C 4-NP + 1.9865, R2 = 0.9924) with 4-NP concentration (0.083–80 µM) (Fig. 4(f)). The detection limit for 4-NP (Fig. 4(g)) is determined to be as low as 83 nM according to the LOD calculation equation: (Tang et al., 2022)
(2)
LOD = 3 σ / k
where σ is the standard deviation of the y-intercepts of regression lines and k is the slope of the calibration graph. It meets well with the maximum allowable content of 0.43 µM in drinking water stipulated by EPA. Thus, this ratiometric strategy is designed for 4-NP sensing with a low LOD and apparent differentiated brightness change under excitations of 390 nm and 468 nm. Moreover, Table S2 summarizes the previous works about utilizing other sensors based on carbon materials for 4-NP determination, and the sensing performance (LOD and detection range) our proposed is comparable or even better than those.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of 4-NP based on 3D printed self-calibrating on-site sensing device
ce:section.ce:para: To extend this sensor into practical applications, we use 3D printing technology to construct a convenient sensing platform for 4-NP accompanied by the smartphone (Fig. 5 (a)). The evaluation is carried out with recovery experiments. 4-NP of different concentrations (0 µM, 15 µM, 30 µM, 45 µM, 60 µM and 75 µM) are dropped in the pretreated BR buffers (Fig. 5(b)), and the mixed solutions are incubated for 2 min. After dripping on the sample inlet, the analyte transfers into the control and test cells. The corresponding lines are regarded as reference and response signals. Notably, lines under the corresponding excitations are differentiated, such as the fluorescence attenuation with the increasing 4-NP concentration at an excitation of 390 nm, while the fluorescence state has almost no apparent variation at an excitation of 468 nm. The PL color and brightness are recorded using a smartphone app to obtain RGB values under two excitation lamps, respectively. A good linear correlation of the Sum2/Sum1 ratio values and 4-NP content is established in Fig. 5(c), showing a wide linear (0.115 to 70 µM) with a LOD of 115 Nm, which means the superior sensing performance of this platform based on the remarkable self-calibrating ability. In addition, to assess the utility of this device in practical samples, 4-NP (10 μM, 30 μM and 60 μM) blended into environmental water samples (sewage, tap water and Yongjiang river water) are determined (Fig. 5(d)) and HPLC is selected as the reference to verify the accuracy of our proposed method. As shown in Table S3, the recoveries (103.5% to 110.8%) and relative standard deviations (RSD, below 4.70%) are reasonable compared with the HPLC technique. Simultaneously, the t-value at the 95% confidence level shows no distinct differences existing in the two strategies, implying a good accuracy of our proposed assay. By performing the repetitive analyte measurements, this platform possesses an excellent repeatability with RSD less than 2.5% (n = 15) (Fig. S9). Moreover, Fig. S10 exhibits the good long-term stability of this sensing platform up to 23 days. These results give evidence of the outstanding feasibility of the 3D printed self-calibrating on-site sensing platform to identify 4-NP in practical samples.

In conclusion, we successfully synthesize an excitation-based ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor for 4-NP monitoring. The nanosensor possesses an innovative concentration-induced bimodal excitation feature, which enables to determine a suitable distance between two excitation peaks (390 nm and 468 nm) for overlapping with the absorption band of 4-NP, thereby triggering off the L-IFE effect and realizing an excitation-based ratiometric method for 4-NP sensing with a LOD of 83 nM in the range of 0.083–80 µM. More meaningfully, a self-calibrating sensing platform is established for the on-the-spot 4-NP determination combining with the above nanosensor features, RGB analysis and 3D printing technology. The as-proposed sensing device, as a multi-technology integration field-testing tool, successfully discriminates the content of 4-NP in practical samples with satisfied recovery rates of 104.3–110.8% and RSD less than 4.70%, paving the way for implementing the application of industrial production of hand-held and consumer-friendly sensors for daily environmental pollution monitoring.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant No. 61604084) and K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University. The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ces.2023.119201.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary video 1
